input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names using built-in framework features.

In .NET 9+, `System.Text.Json` added `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` as a built-in naming policy. Since the record has lowercase parameter names (`name`, `age`), the generated properties will also be lowercase by default in JSON serialization. Using `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` will convert them to `Name` and `Age`.

Wait, let me think about this more carefully. In C#, when you define `public record Person(string name, int age)`, the compiler generates properties `name` and `age` (with lowercase first letters) — actually no. In C#, positional record parameters generate properties with the SAME casing as the parameter names. So `string name` generates a property called `name`, and `int age` generates a property called `age`.


By default, `System.Text.Json` serializes these properties as-is, giving us lowercase names in the JSON output. To convert them to PascalCase (`Name` and `Age`), I need to apply `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` in the serializer options — though I should verify when this policy was actually introduced.

Looking at what's available, `JsonNamingPolicy.CamelCase` has been around since .NET Core 3.0, and the snake case and kebab case variants came in .NET 8. But I'm not certain `PascalCase` exists as a built-in policy — let me think through what naming policies are actually available. The compiler generates properties that match the parameter names exactly, so `name` and `age` would be lowercase properties. I'm trying to figure out whether .NET 11 added a `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` option, but I need to think through the .NET release timeline more carefully — .NET follows a yearly cycle, so .NET 11 would come in November 2026, which might still be in preview depending on the current date. The user wants to leverage the framework's built-in naming policy rather than manually adding attributes, so I should just try creating the project and testing whether `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` exists in .NET 11. If it doesn't work, I can adjust the approach. Let me first check what .NET SDKs are available in this environment.

data.content: Good — .NET 11 preview SDK is available. Let me scaffold the project and try `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase`.
